title: Vellum overlay technique for scrapbooking
subtitle: Layering translucent vellum over photographs and backgrounds
excerpt: Translucent vellum paper laid over a photograph or patterned background mutes and softens what is beneath it while allowing the image to show through. The technique creates visual depth without covering content. Attaching vellum to a scrapbook page requires care because standard adhesives show through the paper as wet marks; this entry covers the adhesive and attachment methods that keep the overlay clean.
sourceNotes: Technique synthesised from scrapbooking practice documented in craft literature of the late 1990s and 2000s, when translucent vellum paper became widely available in hobby supply.

glossaryTerms.definition: A translucent, smooth paper with a slightly waxy surface, used in scrapbooking as an overlay layer. Distinct from genuine vellum (animal skin). Scrapbooking vellum is typically 90-120 gsm and available in plain translucent, frosted, and printed versions.

content.text: softens the layer beneath it. A vivid patterned background becomes muted and distant. A photograph recedes into an impressionistic haze. Used selectively on part of an image, it creates a focal point. What is not covered appears bright and sharp against the softened areas.

attrs.heading: Safety: cutting tools
attrs.body: Use a metal ruler when cutting vellum to size. Vellum is slightly springy and a dull blade will tear the edge rather than slicing it cleanly. Keep fingers behind the ruler and change blades frequently.

items.name: Translucent vellum paper, 90-120 gsm
items.description: Plain translucent works for most applications. Frosted vellum obscures the layer beneath more heavily. Printed vellum (with text or patterns) adds a decorative element while still allowing the background to show.

items.name: Eyelets or brads
items.description: Small metal fasteners punched through the vellum and background to hold the overlay in place without visible adhesive. This is the most common and cleanest method of attaching vellum to a page.

items.name: Bone folder (slug: bone-folder)
items.description: For pressing down edges and scoring fold lines on vellum overlays that wrap a portion of the page.

content.text: Why standard adhesives fail on vellum

PVA glue, paste, and most wet adhesives show through vellum as dark, irregular patches. The wet adhesive is visible because vellum is translucent: any colour or moisture beneath it reads through. Glue stick applied in large areas also shows as a slightly cloudy or matte patch. The solution is to use either mechanical fasteners (eyelets, brads, or staples) or to apply adhesive only at the extreme edges, where it will be hidden by a matt or cropped.

Mark the eyelet positions on the vellum at least 5 mm in from the edge. Use a small hole punch or the tip of a craft knife to pierce a clean hole at each mark. Push a metal eyelet through the hole from the front, then use an eyelet setter and hammer on the back to flatten the eyelet flange. The eyelet grips both the vellum and the background card. Use a minimum of four eyelets: one at each corner: to hold the vellum flat without buckling.
Attaching with dry adhesive at edges only

If eyelets are not available, apply a very thin line of dry adhesive (double-sided tape, 3 mm wide) along the edges of the vellum only, within 3-4 mm of the cut edge. This area will be covered by a mat or the edge of a photo, so the adhesive strip will not be visible. The centre of the vellum sheet floats free, which prevents the adhesive showing through the translucent area.

Vellum cuts cleanly with a sharp craft knife and metal ruler. A dull blade drags and tears the edge rather than slicing it. Vellum can also be torn for a soft, irregular edge: hold the ruler firmly on the vellum and tear upward against the ruler edge in one smooth movement. Scored vellum folds crisply; score on the side that will become the mountain fold (the outside of the crease). Fingerprints show on vellum: handle by the edges only and clean the sheet with a lint-free cloth before mounting.
